1: The false prophet test opens with the possibility that someone claiming divine authorization might lead Israel astray. As a direct pointer to Christ, this verse provides the criteria Jesus himself meets and that his adversaries claimed he violated. The first-century Jewish authorities who condemned Jesus did so under the very categories this chapter establishes -- a miracle-working prophet leading people away from Torah-observance (John 7:12; Deuteronomy 11:47-48). The irony is that Jesus, unlike the false prophets of this chapter, leads Israel toward YHWH rather than away from him.
2: A sign or wonder that actually comes to pass, while the prophet says to follow other gods, establishes that miracles alone do not authenticate a prophet. Jesus explicitly applies this principle: false Christs and false prophets will perform great signs and wonders to deceive even the elect if possible (Matthew 24:24). As a shadow of Christ, this verse establishes the test that Jesus passes: his miracles do not lead to other gods but demonstrate YHWH's own power and presence in Israel (Acts 2:22: Jesus of Nazareth, a man attested to you by God with mighty works and wonders and signs).
3: YHWH is testing you to find out whether you love YHWH your God with all your heart and soul -- the false prophet is an instrument of covenant testing. This is the exact principle at work in Jesus's temptation: Satan offers Jesus a way to obtain all the kingdoms of the world through worship of another power (Matthew 4:8-9), which would have been precisely what Deut 13 describes. Jesus refuses, demonstrating the covenant loyalty that Israel consistently failed to maintain. YHWH uses even false claims to reveal whether the heart is fixed on him.
4: You shall follow YHWH, fear him, keep his commandments, obey his voice, serve him, and hold fast to him -- the comprehensive covenant loyalty demanded in the face of false prophets. This is the loyalty that Jesus demonstrates absolutely: he follows the Father (John 5:19), fears him (Hebrews 5:7), keeps his commandments (John 15:10), obeys his voice (John 8:29), serves him (Matthew 20:28), and holds fast to him even through death. As a theme, Christ is the model of the total covenant loyalty that Israel was commanded to maintain.
5: The false prophet who incites rebellion against YHWH must be put to death -- this is the law under which Jesus was condemned. The charges at Jesus's trial included leading the people astray (Luke 23:2; John 19:7) and blasphemy. The Sanhedrin believed they were applying Deuteronomy 13:5. The resurrection is YHWH's verdict that Jesus was not the false prophet but the true one: the charge was reversed, the death was reversed, and YHWH vindicated the one the authorities condemned. This is not incidental -- Paul calls the crucifixion under Deuteronomy 21:23 and the resurrection YHWH's counter-verdict.
6: If your closest relative -- brother, son, daughter, wife, or dearest friend -- secretly entices you to worship other gods, you shall not consent or listen. Jesus applies this principle in the most radical direction: anyone who loves father or mother, son or daughter, more than me is not worthy of me (Matthew 10:37). The covenant loyalty that supersedes even the closest family bonds in Deuteronomy becomes in Jesus's teaching the call to love him more than family. The claim is the same in both covenants: YHWH's claim overrides every human relationship.
7: The gods of the peoples around you, from one end of the earth to the other, are the temptation of proximity and universality: everyone else is doing it, from nearby and from far away. As a theme, this is the temptation to conformity that Paul addresses in Romans 12:2 (do not be conformed to this age) and that Jesus addresses in the Sermon on the Mount (the broad road that leads to destruction, Matthew 7:13). The universal availability of idolatry does not legitimize it -- and the universal proclamation of Christ (Matthew 28:19) is its antidote.
8: Do not consent to him, do not listen, do not pity, do not spare, do not conceal -- the five prohibitions regarding the family member who entices to idolatry establish the radical priority of covenant loyalty. As a shadow of NT discipleship, this is the teaching Jesus makes explicit: if your hand causes you to sin, cut it off (Matthew 5:30) -- the hyperbolic language of radical severance from anything that draws away from God. The no-pity formula, so jarring to modern ears, is the ancient covenant idiom for maintaining the absolute claim of YHWH over every human relationship.
9: You shall certainly put him to death -- your hand shall be first against him -- establishes that the enticed person must not be passive but must actively participate in the covenant judgment. As a shadow, this radical requirement finds its NT analogue in the call to put to death the deeds of the body by the Spirit (Romans 8:13) and to kill whatever in you is earthly: sexual immorality, impurity, evil desire, covetousness (Colossians 3:5). The active putting to death of what draws away from YHWH is not abrogated in the NT but internalized as spiritual discipline.
10: Stoning the enticer to death because he tried to turn you from YHWH who brought you out of Egypt -- the Exodus redemption is the ground of the covenant claim. As a revelation of God, the Exodus is the basis of YHWH's unique claim on Israel: he redeemed them, therefore they belong to him. The NT grounding is identical: you are not your own; you were bought with a price (1 Corinthians 6:19-20). The seriousness of leading a redeemed person away from their redeemer is proportional to the magnitude of the redemption.
11: All Israel shall hear and be afraid, so that no one does such evil again -- the public deterrence of covenant justice. As a theme, this is the public accountability of the covenant community that the NT continues: those who persist in sin should be rebuked before everyone so that the rest may stand in fear (1 Timothy 5:20). The fear of YHWH is the beginning of wisdom (Proverbs 9:10), and the community that loses it loses its moral compass.
12: If you hear in one of your cities that worthless men have led the inhabitants astray to serve other gods -- the apostate city -- establishes the principle of communal accountability. As a shadow of eschatological judgment, the apostate city that must be devoted to destruction is a type of the final judgment on the nations that have rejected YHWH (Rev 17-18). Jesus's lament over Jerusalem (Matthew 23:37-39; Luke 19:41-44) and his prediction of its destruction anticipates the apostate city judgment, because Jerusalem has rejected the one greater than all the prophets.
13: Worthless men (literally sons of Belial, bene beliya'al) who have gone out from among you -- apostasy that originates within the covenant community -- is the most serious form of religious corruption. As a theme, this corresponds to the NT's warnings about false teachers who arise from within the church (Acts 20:29-30: savage wolves will come in among you, even from your own number). The greatest danger to the covenant community is not external persecution but internal apostasy, which is why both Deuteronomy and the NT NT treat it with maximum severity.
14: Thorough investigation before judgment -- you shall investigate thoroughly and inquire carefully -- establishes the principle of due process before executing the apostate-city judgment. As a revelation of God, this is consistent with YHWH's character throughout the OT: he does not act in judgment without investigation (Genesis 18:20-21: YHWH going down to see if Sodom is as bad as reported). Justice requires evidence, not accusation. The same careful judgment will characterize the final judgment, where every deed is known (Revelation 20:12).
15: Striking down the inhabitants of the apostate city with the sword, devoting it to destruction -- this is the cherem judgment applied to an Israelite city. As a type of final judgment, the devoted-to-destruction language reaches its NT counterpart in Revelation 18:8 (the great Babylon will be burned with fire) and in Jesus's teaching about the cities that refuse the gospel (Matthew 11:20-24: it will be more tolerable for Sodom on the day of judgment than for you). The severity of the apostate-city judgment reflects the severity of the light rejected.
16: Gathering the plunder into the public square and burning the city as a whole burnt offering to YHWH -- the apostate city becomes a sacrifice, not a spoil -- establishes that judgment is not for human benefit but for the vindication of YHWH's covenant. As a shadow, this anticipates the final judgment as YHWH's self-vindication (Romans 3:25-26: God passed over sins to demonstrate his righteousness at the present time). The destruction of the apostate city that enriches no one is a type of the final judgment in which there are no winners except YHWH's justice.
17: None of the devoted things shall cling to your hand, so that YHWH may turn from his burning anger -- the apostate city cannot become personal gain. The connection between covenant integrity and YHWH's turning from anger establishes the mediation pattern: right action on the human side enables YHWH's wrath to subside. As a direct pointer to Christ, this pattern reaches its ultimate expression in the cross: through Christ's complete act of covenant obedience, YHWH's wrath is turned (1 John 2:2; Romans 3:25). No human action can turn YHWH's wrath from sin; only Christ's offering does it.
18: When you obey the voice of YHWH by keeping all his commandments -- the condition for YHWH's compassion and restoration -- establishes the conditional covenant that Christ resolves. Israel could not consistently obey; YHWH's compassion toward his people was therefore always precarious and dependent on their performance. Christ's perfect obedience removes the conditionality: the compassion of God toward his people is now secured in Christ's fulfilled obedience (Romans 8:1: no condemnation for those in Christ Jesus), not dependent on Israel's or the believer's ongoing performance.
